title: CRP
body_html:
CRP Key Points
  • Acute phase protein that is synthesised in the liver.
  • Protein C is not related to CRP.
  • CRP binds to phosphocoline on dead or dying cells and some bacteria activating the compliment system.
  • CRP rises are seen in inflammatory, infectious, malignant conditions and trauma.
  • CRP may also rise in obesity and pregnancy
  • Its half life is approximately 18 hours

Background
CRP is an acute-phase protein, part of the innate immune system. It belongs to the pentraxin family of proteins and is named for its ability to bind the C-polysaccharide of Streptococcus pneumoniae.

Production
  • CRP is synthesised mainly by the liver.
  • Production is stimulated by IL-6 (also IL-1β, TNF-α).

Functions
  • Pattern recognition molecule: binds to phosphocholine on dead/dying cells and pathogens.
  • Opsonisation: promotes phagocytosis by macrophages and neutrophils.
  • Complement activation: via the classical pathway (binds C1q).
  • Modulates inflammation: interacts with Fcγ receptors on immune cells.

Characteristics
  • Non-specific marker of inflammation and tissue injury.
  • Rises within 6–8 hours of an inflammatory stimulus.
  • Peaks at 48 hours, half-life ~18 hours.
  • Falls quickly once inflammation resolves (useful for monitoring).

Normal levels & clinical use
In most UK trusts normal: <5 mg/L

CRP may be used to:
  • Monitor treatment response (e.g., sepsis, pneumonia, autoimmune flare).
  • Some would argue CRP has a use in helping to differentiate bacterial vs viral infections (Bacteria trigger strong IL-6, TNF-α, and IL-1β release from innate immune cells & are associated with more marked rise in CRP than viral infections).
  • Predict cardiovascular risk when measured as hs-CRP (high sensitivity CRP).

500
